title: Caregiver Mental Health Across Generations
abstract: Objective: We evaluated generational differences in caregiver mental and social health to assess the need for targeted interventions for specific generations or age groups. Methods: We utilized cross-sectional 2022 data from a national caregiver survey. Using the cohort-based, life course framework, across n = 537 caregivers and n = 537 demographically matched non-caregivers, we tested the impact of generation, caregiving status, and generation-by-caregiving status interaction on psychological distress, social isolation, and life meaning using ANOVAs. Results: Results revealed significant effects of generation and caregiving status on mental health and social isolation, as well as a generational effect on life meaning (ps < .001). Post-hoc analyses revealed higher psychological distress, social isolation, and lower life meaning scores among Millennial caregivers relative to all other caregiver generations (ps <= 0.021). Conclusions: Younger caregiver generations are at risk for mental and social health outcomes and warrant targeted intervention.
authors_text: Gallagher, Virginia T.; Patterson, Ashleigh; Hebdon, Megan; Manning, Carol A.; Shaffer, Kelly M.
